attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman has just had her first vaginal delivery in the birthing unit. The placenta has been delivered and a perineal examination reveals a small tear involving only the vaginal mucosa and perineal skin. There is no involvement of perineal muscles. There is ongoing bleeding from the tear. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She received epidural anaesthesia during labour which is still effective. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Leave the tear to heal naturally
attributes.explanation:
This patient has sustained a first-degree perineal tear during vaginal delivery, which involves only the vaginal mucosa and perineal skin without damage to the underlying muscle.

Suturing with continuous technique is the most appropriate management for first-degree tears especially with ongoing bleeding. This approach provides good wound approximation, uses less suture material, and creates fewer knots compared to interrupted sutures. Even with effective epidural anaesthesia, local anaesthetic infiltration should still be administered before suturing.

Suturing with interrupted technique is a valid alternative but takes longer to perform and requires more suture material. The multiple knots can cause discomfort during healing and can pose a higher risk for infection.

Leaving the tear to heal naturally may be appropriate to consider in a small first degree tear especially if there is minimal bleeding and the tear is minor. In this case with ongoing bleeding it is more appropriate to repair the tear.

Applying pressure dressing only would be difficult to achieve on a perineum. Whilst pressure can be placed for a short period of time directly with a gauze, pressure dressings can't be effectively applied to a perineum due to the anatomical location. With any concerns for ongoing bleeding from perineal tears it is usually safer to repair the tear.

Topical anaesthetic gel only would provide temporary pain relief but would not address the underlying tissue injury. Proper repair is necessary for optimal healing and prevention of complications.
learningPoints: First-degree perineal tears involve only the vaginal mucosa and perineal skin, not the underlying muscle.
Continuous suturing technique is preferred for first-degree tears as it requires less suture material and fewer knots.
Local anaesthetic infiltration is essential before suturing perineal tears, even if regional anaesthesia was used during labour.
references: Ramar CN, Vadakekut ES, Grimes WR. Perineal lacerations. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 [cited 2024 Nov 21]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559068/
